quick_facts.label: Core purpose
quick_facts.value: A Policy Analyst turns evidence, data and stakeholder inputs into policy research, briefs, recommendations and implementation or monitoring support. In NITI Aayog’s current divisions, this includes policy research and analysis, evidence-based knowledge creation, data analysis, consultations and published policy products.

quick_facts.label: Relevant entry education
quick_facts.value: There is no single mandatory degree for the occupation. Current NITI Aayog Young Professional and consultant criteria accept a relevant master’s degree as one route, alongside specified professional and technical qualifications; specialised public-health training can be especially relevant for health-policy work.

quick_facts.label: MPH connection
quick_facts.value: The National Institute of Health and Family Welfare’s MPH programme explicitly develops analytical/assessment and policy-planning skills for public-health challenges, making an MPH a relevant route into health-policy analysis rather than a universal requirement for all policy domains.

quick_facts.label: UPSC connection
quick_facts.value: UPSC Civil Services Examination preparation can build familiarity with Indian governance and public issues, but analyst roles are separately recruited and typically assess relevant education, experience and analytical capability. UPSC’s Civil Services (Preliminary) Examination 2026 was notified on February 4, 2026, with the preliminary examination held on May 24, 2026.

quick_facts.label: Illustrative public-sector pay context
quick_facts.value: As listed on NITI Aayog’s Work for Viksit Bharat portal when reviewed on July 18, 2026, Young Professional remuneration was ₹70,000 per month; Consultant Grade 1 was listed at ₹80,000–₹1,45,000 per month. These are role-specific contractual figures, not an occupation-wide salary benchmark.

sections.heading: What a Policy Analyst does
paragraphs: Policy Analyst work is evidence-to-decision work. In the Indian public-policy context, responsibilities can include researching policies and comparable practices, interpreting national and sub-national data, preparing policy briefs, background notes, strategy documents and reports, and supporting monitoring tools or programme implementation.

At more experienced levels, work can also involve appraising research proposals and projects, organising consultations and dissemination events, collaborating with ministries, state governments, academic institutions and other experts, and presenting actionable recommendations to decision-makers.
bullets: Conduct quantitative and qualitative research on a policy problem, scheme or sector.
Interpret administrative, survey or other public data; identify gaps, trends and policy implications.
Draft concise briefs, notes, presentations and longer reports for technical and non-technical audiences.
Support monitoring, evaluation, stakeholder consultations and the translation of research into practical recommendations.

sections.heading: Entry routes from MPH and UPSC preparation
paragraphs: An MPH is a strong domain-specific route for health-policy analysis. NIHFW describes its MPH as training professionals to plan, execute and monitor national health policies and programmes, with learning objectives that include collecting and interpreting information and policy planning and development. Graduates should pair this domain training with demonstrable writing, research, data-analysis and field or programme experience.

UPSC preparation is best treated as complementary preparation, not as a credential that automatically qualifies someone for an analyst post. It can help candidates understand constitutional structures, public finance, schemes and current issues; however, current NITI Aayog opportunities specify education, relevant experience and role fit. Build a portfolio through internships, research assistantships, policy writing samples, monitoring-and-evaluation projects or data analysis.

For early experience, NITI Aayog’s opportunities page advertises internships across sectors and states that internship applications are accepted from the 1st through the 10th of each month; applicants should verify the live portal and applicable terms before applying.
bullets: MPH route: specialise in health systems, epidemiology, biostatistics, health financing or programme management; produce policy-relevant research outputs.
UPSC-preparation route: convert subject knowledge into evidence-led briefs, data projects, legislative or scheme analyses and published writing.
General route: pursue a relevant master’s degree or professional qualification, then seek analyst, research-assistant, internship or Young Professional roles aligned to a sector.
Evidence portfolio: maintain writing samples, dashboards or reproducible analyses, research reports, presentations and records of stakeholder or field engagement.

sections.heading: Skills employers and policy teams look for
paragraphs: The role combines subject-matter expertise with analytical and communication capability. NITI Aayog’s current role material refers to data analysis, monitoring and evaluation, policy analytics, policy briefs and strategy documents; its specialist descriptions also emphasise high-quality reports, policy papers and the ability to explain complex issues to non-technical audiences.

The exact technical toolkit varies by sector. For example, public-health analysts may need epidemiological and programme data interpretation, while economic or fiscal analysts may use modelling, public-finance analysis or trade diagnostics. Across domains, clear writing, sound judgement about evidence and collaborative stakeholder work remain central.
bullets: Research design, literature review and assessment of evidence quality.
Quantitative analysis, data cleaning, interpretation and visual communication of results.
Policy writing: briefs, background notes, reports, recommendations and presentations.
Monitoring and evaluation, programme appraisal and implementation awareness.
Stakeholder consultation, collaboration and communication for non-specialist audiences.
Deep domain knowledge in a chosen area, such as health, climate, education, labour, infrastructure, economics or technology.

sections.heading: Where the work happens
paragraphs: Policy Analysts can work in policy-making and advisory teams that connect research to government decisions, programme design and implementation. NITI Aayog provides a concrete public-sector example: its divisions cover areas including health, economics and finance, skills and employment, public finance and policy analysis, water and land resources, law, climate and data management.

The day-to-day setting is therefore often cross-functional rather than purely desk-based: analysts work with government departments, state governments, researchers and domain experts, and may contribute to consultation processes, workshops, project reviews and policy publications.
bullets: Government policy institutions and programme-monitoring or evaluation teams.
Sector-focused policy divisions, for example health, skills, economic policy, law, climate or public finance.
Research, data-management and inter-institutional collaboration environments.
Roles involving coordination with ministries, states, research institutions and technical experts.

sections.heading: Career progression
paragraphs: A typical progression is from internship, research assistant or entry-level analyst work into a Young Professional or junior analyst role, followed by consultant or specialist responsibilities as a candidate develops a domain record, stronger research outputs and experience managing policy workstreams. Titles and thresholds vary by organisation and are not a guaranteed ladder.

As an illustrative current public-sector framework, NITI Aayog’s portal lists Young Professional roles with at least one year of experience, Consultant Grade 1 with three to eight years, Consultant Grade 2 with eight to fifteen years, and Senior Consultant with fifteen-plus years. Senior roles are framed around policy formulation, evaluation and strategic leadership.
bullets: Early career: internships, research assistance, data and programme support, supervised policy writing.
Developing analyst: independent briefs and analyses, stakeholder coordination, monitoring and evaluation responsibilities.
Experienced consultant or specialist: lead thematic research, shape strategy, supervise workstreams and advise senior stakeholders.
Senior leadership: set policy-research agendas, direct evaluation or implementation portfolios, and manage multidisciplinary teams.

sections.heading: Compensation context
paragraphs: Compensation is highly variable by employer, sector, city, employment type and prior experience. For a dated official reference point rather than a market-wide estimate, NITI Aayog’s Work for Viksit Bharat portal listed monthly remuneration of ₹70,000 for Young Professionals, ₹80,000–₹1,45,000 for Consultant Grade 1, ₹1,45,000–₹2,65,000 for Consultant Grade 2, and ₹2,65,000–₹3,30,000 for Senior Consultants when reviewed on July 18, 2026.

These figures should be read as NITI Aayog’s listed remuneration for its own engagement categories and associated eligibility/experience bands. They do not establish pay at think tanks, consulting firms, universities, NGOs, state bodies or multilateral organisations, where compensation and contract terms can differ substantially.
bullets: Use live vacancy notices to compare whether pay is consolidated remuneration, a salary scale or a consultancy fee.
Compare the stated tenure, benefits, location requirements, travel expectations and experience threshold—not only the headline monthly figure.
Treat all compensation information as time-sensitive and employer-specific.
